tweets.text:
My top picks across #uranium, #silver, #copper, #potash, #fluorspar, and #graphene 👇

These are not necessarily the safest investments. They are simply the companies where I currently see the most attractive risk/reward.

☢️ URANIUM

Producers & Developers
$CCJ – The blue-chip uranium name.
$KAP – Lowest-cost producer in the world.
$DNN – Fully permitted and advancing Wheeler River.
$NXE – One of the world's largest undeveloped uranium projects.
$UUUU – Received a conditional US government loan commitment of up to $725 million to expand domestic rare earth processing and strengthen critical mineral supply chains. Exposure to uranium, rare earths, and growing US strategic support.
$EU – Current U.S. production.
$UEC – Unhedged and ramping up production.
Explorers
$SYH – Drilling near Wheeler River and one of the best prospect generators in the Athabasca Basin.
$M.CN – Potentially the largest uranium deposit in the U.S. based on historic drilling and recent radiometric results. 655 Mlbs endowment in the area. Drills turning, stay tuned for news!
$NOBL – Recently published a maiden resource at Duck Creek with significant expansion potential.
$FUU – The team behind four discoveries. If JR and Tetra connect, this could become a monster.

🥈 SILVER
I own three established producers with great margins at current silver prices:
$HL
$AG
$PAAS
I also own these three explorers:
$HYMC – Drilling one of the largest precious metals systems in the world.
$GRSL.V – Recently reported a standout drill intercept.
$JTWO – My wildcard. Bonanza-grade silver samples suggest a much larger high-grade system.

🔌 COPPER
$CGNT – My primary copper exposure. A massive deposit with significant leverage to higher copper prices.

🌾 POTASH
$KCLI – Massive exploration target in Utah's Paradox Basin with district-scale potential. Drilling to start within weeks!

💎 FLUORSPAR
$CTV.V – CleanTech Vanadium is moving from planning to execution. A major drill program at its Campbell-Crotser fluorspar project in Kentucky is planned, targeting material for an initial underground mine plan and a planned 500 tpd processing operation.

🌐 GRAPHENE 
$HGRAF – Still my largest single holding. A unique graphene technology platform with potential applications across multiple industries.

Disclosure: I have paid partnerships with Myriad Uranium, Skyharbour Resources, American Critical Minerals, F3 Uranium and CleanTech Vanadium.

Not financial advice. Please do your own due diligence.

tweets.text:
#Uranium #Silver #Gold #Oil

My Sunday thoughts...

The Strait of Hormuz seems to be opening and closing like a revolving door.

If an agreement is reached to keep Hormuz open, much of today's geopolitical risk premium in oil will likely fade.

If shipping is disrupted for several more weeks ahead, we're suddenly talking about a very different macro environment.

🛢️ Oil: The obvious winner. But the Trump administration will do everything it can to prevent a sustained spike in oil prices. It is questionable however whether they will succeed. Strategic reserves are not indefinite.

🥇 Gold: Medium and longer term I'm constructive, but recent history shows that during market panics even gold can be sold as investors raise cash.

🥈 Silver: The same liquidity risk applies, perhaps even more so because of its industrial exposure. However, silver continues to face a structural supply deficit, with demand consistently exceeding newly mined supply. If markets move beyond the initial panic and refocus on inflation, monetary policy and fundamentals, silver could become one of the biggest beneficiaries.

☢️ Uranium: A spike in oil prices won't immediately increase uranium demand, but it does reinforce one of the world's biggest investment themes: energy security. Add to that the enormous electricity demand from AI and data centers, continued global electrification, and growing concerns over sulfuric acid availability—a critical input for ISR uranium production—and I believe the long-term fundamentals for uranium continue to strengthen.

Short-term liquidity drives markets.

Long-term fundamentals drive returns. 

That's why I am still very bullish precious metals and uranium. Visit my profile for top picks. Please do your own research.

tweets.text:
People don’t appreciate enough how easy it is to pick winners right now.

The best thing about this horrible market over the last 1-2 years is that you can finally see who actually means it.

Anyone can build when the chart is green and the whole market is flying. What people take for granted is how hard it is to keep going when your token is down -90%, your community and investors turn on you, and every excuse to quit is sitting right there.

The founders still standing after a market like this passed one of the toughest filters there is. And that filter tells you almost everything you need to know about who’s worth backing.

tweets.text:
Most BTCFi products are built for the wrong type of Bitcoin holder.

The biggest holders won’t give up custody, no matter how good the yield looks.

Muneeb made this exact point here. Everyone talks about bringing BTC into DeFi like Bitcoin holders are one group.

They're not because Institutions, treasuries, and OG holders usually don’t want to give up custody just to earn yield.

$sBTC helps bring Bitcoin into the onchain economy for people who are comfortable using DeFi.

But self-custodial BTC staking is the bigger institutional unlock, because it lets holders keep custody of their Bitcoin while still earning BTC-denominated yield.

And the early base is already forming:

• sBTC hit a $545M TVL peak
• $121M deployed across Stacks DeFi
• Zest has $75.9M TVL in BTC lending
• Bitflow did $67.4M volume in Q1
• Hermetica hBTC showed 5.60% 7-day APY

Honestly, the first lane was always gonna get built.

It's the custody-first lane that decides whether the trillion-plus in idle BTC ever does anything.

Glad to partner with @Stacks to highlight this one.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Trust built onchain has a way of moving people from watching to trying.

Stacks is the home for Bitcoin-native finance. https://t.co/oDmO5ay01r

tweets.text:
VAR is really ruining  a lot of the World Cup experience.  If we take it to an extreme, have Fable or Chat be an Ai ref analyzing in real time every second of the game, it would constantly be calling fouls, giving yellows, etc.  so either do that and really ruin the game or let it be selectively used like now and have half the fan base thinking the fix is in!  

Turn off the machines and let the boys play!!!

tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
derek thompsons recent article inspired me to read more about alcohol

im shocked by the est impact vs current narrative

for instance, 7-14 drinks per wk reduces life expectancy by only 6 mos (!!)

moreover, the negative effect is possibly offset by gains from deeper friendships

tweets.text:
Large neural networks are a bit like black holes for know-how. The more useful you want them to be, the more you feed them: your workflows, your corrections, your evals, your judgment, your taste, your edge…

And once that knowledge is absorbed into someone else’s learning loop, the model can start replicating the intelligence without needing the people or organization that created it in the first place.

That is the core issue Satya is addressing. In the AI era, companies don’t just accumulate data. They accumulate learning. The real IP is no longer only in documents, databases, patents, or codebases. It is in the traces: prompts, feedback, corrections, evals, agent actions, memories, adapted weights.

Every correction is a tiny transfer of human capital into token capital.

This is why enterprises will care so much about owning their learning loop. Not just “is my data protected?” but: who owns the intelligence created when my people use AI every day?

Because today in consuming intelligence, you are creating intelligence. And what you create should belong to you.

Not your weights, not your edge.

And once that knowledge is absorbed into someone else’s learning loop, the model can start replicating the intelligence without needing the people or organization that created it in the first place.

That is the core issue Satya is addressing. In the AI era, companies don’t just accumulate data. They accumulate learning. The real IP is no longer only in documents, databases, patents, or codebases. It is in the traces: prompts, feedback, corrections, evals, agent actions, memories, adapted weights.

Every correction is a tiny transfer of human capital into token capital.

This is why enterprises will care so much about owning their learning loop. Not just “is my data protected?” but: who owns the intelligence created when my people use AI every day?

Because today in consuming intelligence, you are creating intelligence. And what you create should belong to you.
